attributes.stem: An otherwise well 32-year-old woman is admitted for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She is very fit, has never smoked, does not drink alcohol and is not on any medications, except the oral contraceptive pill. Her BMI is 22. It is anticipated that the time under anaesthesia will be about 30 minutes. There is no history or family history of venous thromboembolism. Consideration is given to any measures that might need to be taken to minimise the risk of any venous thromboembolic complications.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to intra-operative pneumatic calf compression, which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: No other specific measures are required
Cease contraceptive and defer the procedure
Postoperative enoxaparin for one week after the procedure
Postoperative aspirin for one week after the procedure
attributes.explanation:
The patient in question is at low risk of perioperative DVT and undergoing a relatively minor procedure (estimated <45 minutes) which is unlikely to affect postoperative mobility. The best-validated perioperative DVT risk assessment tool is the Caprini score, with patients scoring >6 considered at risk of perioperative DVT indicating chemoprophylaxis. This patient scores one on the Caprini risk score (oral contraceptive pill) and so is at low risk and does not need any other prophylactic measures. She can continue taking the pill and there is no need to defer the procedure.

As this patient is not sufficiently high-risk to warrant chemoprophylaxis, no other specific measures are required. Inappropriate perioperative use of chemoprophylaxis can lead to adverse outcomes, most notably increased risk of postoperative bleeding.

Postoperative enoxaparin, either for one week or until discharge, is not indicated for the reasons above. Moderate-risk patients may receive enoxaparin until discharge, with high-risk patients receiving enoxaparin for 7-10 days or longer depending on their risk stratification. However, in the real world, such a patient would very probably be given enoxaparin until discharge - on the premise that the operation may turn out to be more major than anticipated. That is not usually the case.

Postoperative aspirin is not typically used as it does not confer sufficient prophylactic benefit against DVT.
learningPoints: The Caprini score is the best-validated risk assessment tool for perioperative DVT risk.
Patients at a low risk of DVT undergoing minor procedures do not require DVT chemoprophylaxis.
references: Pannucci CJ, Swistun L, MacDonald JK, Henke PK, Brooke BS. Individualized Venous Thromboembolism Risk Stratification Using the 2005 Caprini Score to Identify the Benefits and Harms of Chemoprophylaxis in Surgical Patients: A Meta-analysis. Ann Surg. 2017 Jun;265(6):1094-1103. doi: 10.1097/SLA.0000000000002126. PMID: 28106607.
Guideline for the Prevention of Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) in Adult Hospitalised Patients. https://www.health.qld.gov.au/clinical- practice/guidelines-procedures/medicines/safety
